content:
What We Do Japan Profiler analyzes hundreds of real traveler and resident experiences — from Reddit threads, forums, and community discussions — to identify the problems that existing guides don’t solve.
Every article is built on data, not just personal experience. We look at what people actually struggle with, verify the facts, and create guides with practical solutions.
Why We Exist There’s no shortage of Japan travel content online. But most of it covers the same topics the same way: “Top 10 Things to Do in Tokyo,” “Best Restaurants in Kyoto.”
Meanwhile, real travelers are stuck searching for answers to specific, frustrating problems:
Why won’t my Mastercard charge Suica? How do I actually get to Suzuka Circuit for the F1? Is this private tour guide legitimate or a scam? These are the questions we answer.
Our Approach Identify real pain points from communities where people share unfiltered experiences Research deeply — industry structure, pricing data, competitive landscape Write actionable guides with specific steps, not generic advice Update regularly as situations change Contact Have a topic you’d like us to investigate? Reach out — we’re always looking for the next problem worth solving.
summary:
What We Do

Japan Profiler analyzes hundreds of real traveler and resident experiences — from Reddit threads, forums, and community discussions — to identify the problems that existing guides don’t solve.

Every article is built on data, not just personal experience. We look at what people actually struggle with, verify the facts, and create guides with practical solutions.

Why We Exist

There’s no shortage of Japan travel content online. But most of it covers the same topics the same way: “Top 10 Things to Do in Tokyo,” “Best Restaurants in Kyoto.”
title: About Japan Profiler

content:
A Reddit user paid for a “private guided tour” in Kyoto. What they got was a driver who spoke minimal English, had zero knowledge of the temples they visited, and offered no cultural commentary whatsoever. The post got 332 upvotes and hundreds of comments from travelers with similar stories.
This isn’t an isolated case. As Japan’s tourism boom accelerates — 36.87 million foreign visitors in 2024 alone — a growing number of travelers are discovering that the “private guide” they booked is nothing like what was advertised.
Here’s what you need to know before you book, and how to protect yourself.
Why This Problem Is Getting Worse Three things collided:
1. The tourism explosion
Japan welcomed 36.87 million visitors in 2024, surpassing the pre-pandemic record by 16%. Tourist spending hit ¥8.1 trillion (USD $53.3 billion) — a 53% jump from the previous year. The numbers keep climbing.
2. Not enough licensed guides
Japan has 27,277 registered licensed guide interpreters (通訳案内士). But only 20–30% of them are actively working. That leaves roughly 6,000 active licensed guides for nearly 37 million visitors — about 1 guide for every 6,000 tourists.
And 75% of those guides are concentrated in Tokyo and Osaka, leaving Kyoto, Hiroshima, and rural destinations severely underserved.
3. The 2018 law change
Before January 2018, it was technically illegal to offer paid foreign-language guiding without a national license. After the reform, anyone can legally charge money to guide tourists — no license, no exam, no background check required.
This was a necessary change. Japan simply didn’t have enough licensed guides to meet demand. But it also removed the quality floor. The only restriction that remains: unlicensed guides cannot call themselves “National Government Licensed Guide Interpreters.” Everything else is fair game.
The 5 Most Common Scam Patterns 1. The Driver Bait-and-Switch What happens: You book a “private tour with English-speaking guide.” What shows up is a driver who speaks minimal English, has no guiding knowledge, and simply takes you from point A to point B.
Real example: The Alpha Tours Japan incident in Kyoto. The customer paid for a guided experience and received a chauffeur-only service with no cultural commentary, no itinerary management, and no meaningful language support.
How to spot it: If the booking page doesn’t show the specific guide’s profile, bio, and reviews — be cautious. Legitimate guide platforms let you see exactly who will be guiding you.
2. The Vague Itinerary What happens: The tour is listed as “Kyoto Highlights” or “Tokyo Best Spots” with no specific temples, timing, or logistics mentioned. After you pay, the “tour” turns out to be a generic drive-by of popular spots.
How to spot it: Ask for a detailed, hour-by-hour itinerary before paying. A real guide will happily provide one — it’s how they demonstrate their expertise. If they say “we’ll decide on the day,” that’s a red flag.
3. The No-Show What happens: You arrive at the meeting point and the guide never shows. You call the platform’s customer service and get put on hold, or told the booking is “locked” and not immediately refundable.
Real examples: Documented on Viator (Mt. Fuji tour no-show) and multiple GetYourGuide threads.
How to protect yourself: Book through platforms that offer real-time messaging with guides. If you can’t contact the guide directly before the tour, you have no backup if they don’t appear.
4. The “Place Is Closed” Redirect What happens: Your guide tells you a famous landmark is “unexpectedly closed today” and redirects you to an alternative location — typically a souvenir shop or restaurant where they earn a commission.
How to spot it: Check the landmark’s hours yourself before the tour. Most temples and attractions in Japan post real-time status online. If a guide insists something is closed and pushes an alternative, verify independently.
5. Fake Credentials What happens: Near popular tourist spots, individuals approach you wearing official-looking badges, claiming to be certified guides. They offer cheap tours that inevitably end at overpriced souvenir shops.
How to spot it: Legitimate guides don’t approach strangers at train stations. They have pre-booked clients. If someone approaches you unsolicited offering a tour, decline.
How Much Should a Private Guide Actually Cost? One of the best defenses against getting scammed is knowing the real market rate.
Service Typical Price Range Hourly rate (per group) $35–45/hour Half-day tour (4 hours) $150–250/group Full-day tour (7–8 hours) $200–400/group Licensed guide premium +20–50% above unlicensed rates Red flags:
A full-day “private guided tour” in Tokyo or Kyoto for under $100? Something is likely missing from the service. Over $600 for a full day without transport included? You’re probably being overcharged. Note: Tipping is not customary in Japan. A small gift or thank-you note is appreciated but never expected.
Licensed vs. Unlicensed: What You Actually Need to Know This is where most travel advice gets it wrong.
Licensed (通訳案内士) does NOT automatically mean good. The exam tests factual knowledge — historical dates, geographic trivia, tourism regulations. It does not test storytelling ability, personality, or practical guiding experience. And 75% of license holders aren’t even actively guiding.
Unlicensed does NOT automatically mean bad. Many of Japan’s best-reviewed guides on platforms like GoWithGuide and MagicalTrip are unlicensed. They may be passionate locals, retired professionals, or long-term expats with deep knowledge of their neighborhood.
What actually matters:
Years of guiding experience Reviews from real clients (mentioning the guide by name) Depth of knowledge about the specific area you’re visiting Communication style and responsiveness before booking A detailed itinerary provided upfront The license is one data point, not a guarantee.
Where to Book: Platform Comparison Platform Best For Watch Out For GoWithGuide Direct guide selection, video profiles, founded in Tokyo Smaller selection in rural areas MagicalTrip Curated local experiences, TripAdvisor 2025 Best of the Best Less customization for itineraries Viator Huge selection, lots of reviews Documented no-show issues, refund difficulties GetYourGuide Curated experiences, good UX Customer service complaints, double-charging reports Klook Last-minute deals, attraction tickets Less guide vetting, activity-focused Japan Guide Agency All guides are nationally licensed Higher price, less flexibility The safest option in any platform: Book a specific, named guide — not a company. If the listing only shows a company name and no individual guide profile, you have no way to know who will actually show up.
Your Pre-Booking Checklist Before you pay for any private guide in Japan, verify these 8 things:
Can you see the specific guide’s profile? Not just a company page — the actual person. Is there a video of them speaking? This instantly verifies language ability. Do reviews mention the guide by name? Generic company reviews are less reliable. Did they provide a detailed itinerary before payment? Hour-by-hour, with specific locations. Can you message them directly before booking? This is your single best protection. Is the cancellation and refund policy in writing? Read it before you pay. Is the pricing within market range? $200–400 for a full day is normal. Way below or above = investigate. Do they respond promptly and specifically? Vague or delayed responses before booking predict vague service during the tour. If a listing fails 3 or more of these checks, keep looking. There are thousands of excellent guides in Japan — you don’t need to gamble on a questionable one.
The Bottom Line Japan remains one of the safest and most rewarding travel destinations in the world. The vast majority of tour experiences are excellent. But the combination of record-breaking tourism demand, a 2018 law change that removed licensing requirements, and not enough quality guides to go around has created gaps that bad actors are exploiting.
The good news: the scams follow predictable patterns, and they’re avoidable with basic due diligence. Use the checklist above, book through platforms that show individual guide profiles, and trust your instincts — if a deal seems too good to be true in Japan, it probably is.
Have you had a great (or terrible) experience with a private guide in Japan? We’d love to hear your story — it helps other travelers make better decisions.
Sources & Further Reading Japan Tourism Agency: National Licensed Guide Interpreter System GoWithGuide: How Much Is a Local Private Guide in Tokyo Nippon.com: International Visitor Spending Surges to ¥8 Trillion Unseen Japan: Are More Tourists Getting Scammed in Japan?
summary:
A Reddit user paid for a “private guided tour” in Kyoto. What they got was a driver who spoke minimal English, had zero knowledge of the temples they visited, and offered no cultural commentary whatsoever. The post got 332 upvotes and hundreds of comments from travelers with similar stories.

This isn’t an isolated case. As Japan’s tourism boom accelerates — 36.87 million foreign visitors in 2024 alone — a growing number of travelers are discovering that the “private guide” they booked is nothing like what was advertised.
title: Private Tour Guides in Japan: How to Avoid Getting Scammed (2026 Guide)

content:
So you’ve got tattoos and you want to soak in a steaming outdoor hot spring surrounded by misty mountains in the Japanese countryside. Sounds dreamy, right? Then you do a quick Google search and – boom – reality check. Most onsens in Japan have a strict no-tattoo policy. Your sleeve of flowers or that little ankle piece? Same deal. No entry.
But here’s the thing: Japan’s relationship with tattoos and onsens is slowly changing. And if you’re willing to look beyond the obvious tourist spots, there are genuine hidden gems in rural Japan where nobody cares about your ink. You just need to know where to look.
I’ve put together this guide to help you find those places – real onsens in the Japanese countryside where you can soak without stress, without cover-up patches, and without that awkward moment at the front desk. Let’s get into it.
Why Most Onsens Ban Tattoos Before we jump into the list, let’s quickly talk about why this is even a thing.
In Japan, tattoos have historically been associated with yakuza – the Japanese organized crime groups. Full-body irezumi were a defining visual marker of yakuza membership for decades. Because of this, many onsen operators started banning tattooed guests in the 1990s and 2000s to keep yakuza out and make other guests comfortable.
Japan knows most foreign visitors with tattoos aren’t yakuza. The Japan Tourism Agency conducted a survey in 2015 and encouraged onsens to accommodate tattooed visitors. Some responded by allowing small tattoos, offering cover-up stickers, or designating specific hours.
Still, most traditional onsens maintain the ban. That’s why rural areas and smaller family-run establishments are your best bet – they tend to be more flexible.
How to Find Tattoo-Friendly Onsens Here are your best strategies:
Check Tattoo-Friendly Databases. The website tattoo-friendly.jp maintains a searchable list of facilities across Japan. The Japan National Tourism Organization (JNTO) also provides information.
Call Ahead. The single most reliable method. Call the onsen directly (or ask your hotel to call) and ask: “Tattoo ga arimasu ga, daijoubu desu ka?” (I have tattoos, is that okay?). Policies change without notice, so always confirm.
Look for Private Baths (Kashikiri Buro). Many onsens that ban tattoos in public baths offer private baths for 1,000-5,000 yen per session. Full onsen experience, total privacy.
Try Ryokans with In-Room Baths. Some rural ryokans have rooms with private onsen baths attached. Since it’s your own room, tattoo policies don’t apply.
Bring Cover-Up Patches. Some facilities allow entry if you cover tattoos with waterproof adhesive patches, sold at drugstores and Don Quijote. Works best for smaller tattoos.
1. Takaragawa Onsen (Minakami, Gunma Prefecture) Located deep in the mountains of Gunma Prefecture, this sprawling riverside complex features four massive open-air baths along the Takaragawa River. Three are mixed-gender (konyoku) and one is women-only.
Takaragawa has been widely reported as tattoo-friendly, confirmed by numerous visitor reports and travel publications.
Location: Minakami-machi, Tone-gun, Gunma Prefecture Tattoo Policy: Tattoos accepted (confirmed by multiple visitor reports; verify current policy before visiting) Admission: Approximately 1,500-2,000 yen for day-use bathing (verify current pricing) Access: From Tokyo, take the Joetsu Shinkansen to Jomo-Kogen Station (approximately 70 minutes), then a bus or taxi to the onsen (approximately 30 minutes) What Makes It Special: The sheer scale of the outdoor baths is breathtaking. Massive boulders, old-growth trees, and the sound of the river rushing by. In winter, the snow-covered landscape is stunning. Ryokan on-site for overnight stays.
Pro Tip: Go on a weekday if possible. This place is popular with day-trippers from Tokyo on weekends.
2. Kurokawa Onsen (Minami-Oguni, Kumamoto Prefecture) A charming hot spring town tucked into a narrow valley in Kumamoto Prefecture, Kyushu. About 30 ryokans with a beautifully preserved atmosphere – wooden buildings, stone paths, and lanterns.
Many ryokans here are reported as tattoo-friendly, particularly for foreign visitors. Policies vary by ryokan, so always check with your specific accommodation.
Location: Minami-Oguni-machi, Aso-gun, Kumamoto Prefecture Tattoo Policy: Many facilities accommodate tattooed guests; confirm with individual ryokan before booking Admission: Day-use bathing varies by ryokan (typically 500-800 yen). The “Nyuto Tegata” bath-hopping pass allows you to visit three different ryokan baths for 1,300 yen (verify current pricing) Access: From Kumamoto City, take a bus to Kurokawa Onsen (approximately 3 hours). From Fukuoka, highway buses run directly (approximately 2.5 hours) What Makes It Special: Outdoor baths carved into rocky hillsides, surrounded by dense forest. The bath-hopping pass lets you try multiple onsens in one visit. The town is tiny and incredibly atmospheric – especially at dusk when the lanterns are lit.
Pro Tip: Get the bath-hopping pass. Each ryokan’s bath has a different character, and walking between them is half the fun.
3. Nyuto Onsen-kyo (Semboku, Akita Prefecture) If you want to feel like you’ve time-traveled to old Japan, Nyuto Onsen-kyo is your place. This cluster of seven secluded hot spring lodges sits in the mountains near Lake Tazawa in Akita Prefecture, deep in the Tohoku region. Some of these lodges have been operating for over 300 years.
Several of the ryokans in Nyuto Onsen-kyo are reported to accept tattooed guests. Tsurunoyu, the most famous of the seven, is particularly well-known among international visitors. However, as with any rural onsen area, policies are set by individual operators, so always confirm in advance.
Location: Semboku-shi, Akita Prefecture Tattoo Policy: Several facilities accept tattoos; confirm with specific lodge before visiting Admission: Day-use bathing approximately 500-800 yen depending on facility (verify current pricing) Access: From Tokyo, take the Akita Shinkansen to Tazawako Station (approximately 3 hours), then a local bus (approximately 40-50 minutes) What Makes It Special: The milky-white sulfur water at Tsurunoyu is legendary. The mixed-gender outdoor bath feels completely untouched by modern life. Rustic wooden buildings, thatched roofs, and kerosene lamps – one of the most atmospheric onsens in Japan.
Pro Tip: Stay overnight if you can. The evening and early morning bathing experiences – when the day visitors have left – are magical.
4. Sukayu Onsen (Aomori, Aomori Prefecture) Sukayu Onsen is famous for one thing: its massive Hiba Sennin Buro – a single indoor bath made entirely of hiba wood that can fit 1,000 people. Yes, you read that right. The bath hall is enormous, and the whole place smells incredible thanks to the aromatic hiba cypress wood.
Located on the slopes of Mount Hakkoda in Aomori Prefecture, Sukayu has been a healing retreat (toji) for centuries. The onsen has been reported by multiple travelers as tattoo-friendly, welcoming both domestic and international visitors with tattoos.
Location: Aomori-shi, Aomori Prefecture (on the slopes of Mount Hakkoda) Tattoo Policy: Tattoos reported as accepted by visitor accounts; confirm current policy before visiting Admission: Approximately 1,000 yen for day-use (verify current pricing) Access: From Aomori Station, take a JR bus toward Mount Hakkoda (approximately 70 minutes) What Makes It Special: The Sennin Buro is unlike anything else in Japan. The massive wooden bath hall has a cathedral-like quality, and the strong sulfur waters are said to cure everything from muscle pain to skin conditions. Mixed-gender with a dividing line, plus separate bathing times available.
Pro Tip: Visit in winter. Sukayu holds the record for most snowfall in Japan. Soaking in sulfur water surrounded by meters of snow is unforgettable.
5. Yunomine Onsen (Tanabe, Wakayama Prefecture) Yunomine Onsen is one of the oldest hot springs in Japan, with a history stretching back over 1,800 years. It’s a tiny village along the Kumano Kodo pilgrimage route in Wakayama Prefecture. The star attraction is Tsuboyu, a small natural cave bath that is a UNESCO World Heritage Site – the only hot spring bath in the world with that designation.
Yunomine is a small, traditional village with a relaxed atmosphere. The public bath facilities in the village have been reported as accommodating to tattooed visitors, particularly foreign pilgrims hiking the Kumano Kodo. Tsuboyu is a private bath by nature (it fits only 2-3 people), so tattoo policies are irrelevant there.
Location: Hongu-cho, Tanabe-shi, Wakayama Prefecture Tattoo Policy: Village baths reported as tattoo-accommodating; Tsuboyu is a private bath (tattoo-irrelevant). Confirm current policy before visiting Admission: Tsuboyu approximately 800 yen per 30-minute session; public bath approximately 400 yen (verify current pricing) Access: From Tanabe or Shingu, take a bus toward Hongu (approximately 1.5-2 hours from either direction) What Makes It Special: You crouch into a small natural cave, and the water changes color throughout the day – from clear to blue to milky white. The village also has a public cooking area where locals boil eggs and vegetables in the hot spring water.
Pro Tip: Book Tsuboyu early or be prepared to wait. Sessions are 30 minutes, and there’s often a queue, especially during peak hiking season on the Kumano Kodo (spring and autumn).
6. Shirahone Onsen (Matsumoto, Nagano Prefecture) Hidden in the mountains between Matsumoto and Takayama in the Japanese Alps, Shirahone Onsen is known for its distinctive milky-white water. The name literally means “white bone” – referring to the water’s opaque white color caused by dissolved minerals. There’s a famous local saying: “If you bathe here for three days, you won’t catch a cold for three years.”
Shirahone is a small, quiet onsen village with a handful of ryokans. Several have been reported by international visitors as tattoo-friendly. The remote location and small scale of operations generally means a more relaxed attitude compared to larger commercial facilities.
Location: Azumi, Matsumoto-shi, Nagano Prefecture Tattoo Policy: Several ryokans reported as tattoo-accommodating; confirm with specific facility before visiting Admission: Day-use bathing varies by ryokan (typically 500-1,000 yen; verify current pricing) Access: From Matsumoto, take a bus toward Norikura/Shirahone Onsen (approximately 1.5 hours). From Takayama, it’s accessible via Hirayu Onsen with a bus connection What Makes It Special: The milky white water contrasts beautifully against dark rocks and green forest. Great for skin health, and the area is a gateway to the Northern Alps for combining onsen bathing with mountain hiking.
Pro Tip: In winter, the road access can be limited due to heavy snowfall. Check road conditions before heading out, and consider staying overnight rather than attempting a day trip.
7. Beppu Onsen (Beppu, Oita Prefecture) Beppu is the undisputed hot spring capital of Japan. This city on the eastern coast of Kyushu produces more hot spring water than any other city in the world (second globally only to Yellowstone). With eight distinct hot spring areas (known as “Beppu Hatto”), thousands of hot spring vents, and an incredible variety of water types, Beppu is a paradise for onsen lovers.
While Beppu is a city rather than a single rural facility, it earns a spot here because of its progressive tattoo policies. The city has actively worked to welcome international visitors, and many facilities accept tattooed guests.
Location: Beppu-shi, Oita Prefecture, Kyushu Tattoo Policy: Many facilities accept tattoos; the city actively promotes tattoo-friendly bathing. Check with individual facilities for current policy Admission: Public baths (jito) can be as cheap as 100-300 yen. Day-use facilities typically 500-1,500 yen (verify current pricing) Access: From Fukuoka, take the JR Sonic limited express to Beppu (approximately 2 hours). Beppu also has a small airport with domestic flights What Makes It Special: The variety is unmatched. Sand baths, mud baths, blue-tinged water, iron-rich red water – you could spend a week here with a different experience every day. The “Jigoku Meguri” (Hell Tour) of boiling hot springs is a must-see attraction (viewing only, not bathing).
Pro Tip: Start with the local public baths (called “jito” in the Beppu dialect). Insanely cheap, used by locals daily. Takegawara Onsen, a Meiji-era wooden bathhouse, offers sand bathing and is reported as tattoo-friendly.
Tips for Visiting Onsens with Tattoos Here are practical tips for a smooth experience:
Always Confirm Before You Go. Policies change. A 30-second phone call can save you hours of travel and disappointment.
Learn Basic Onsen Etiquette. Wash thoroughly before entering the bath. Don’t put your towel in the water. Don’t swim or splash. Keep your voice down. Following etiquette shows respect and makes everyone comfortable.
Bring Tattoo Covers Just in Case. Waterproof cover patches from drugstores or Don Quijote are smart insurance, even at tattoo-friendly places.
Consider the Timing. Early morning, late evening, or weekdays mean fewer people and a more relaxed atmosphere.
Be Respectful if Turned Away. Don’t argue. Thank the staff politely and move to your backup option.
Private Baths Are Always an Option. When in doubt, book a kashikiri (private) bath. Same water, same views, complete privacy.
Final Thoughts Japan’s onsen culture is one of the most wonderful things about the country, and having tattoos shouldn’t mean you miss out. The landscape is shifting – slowly, but it is shifting. More facilities are relaxing their policies, and more Japanese people understand that foreign tattoos carry different cultural meanings.
From Takaragawa’s riverside baths to Yunomine’s ancient cave bath, from snowy Shirahone to steaming Beppu – these places are worth the trip, tattoos or not. Do your homework, make that phone call, and go soak. You deserve it.
summary:
So you’ve got tattoos and you want to soak in a steaming outdoor hot spring surrounded by misty mountains in the Japanese countryside. Sounds dreamy, right? Then you do a quick Google search and – boom – reality check. Most onsens in Japan have a strict no-tattoo policy. Your sleeve of flowers or that little ankle piece? Same deal. No entry.

But here’s the thing: Japan’s relationship with tattoos and onsens is slowly changing. And if you’re willing to look beyond the obvious tourist spots, there are genuine hidden gems in rural Japan where nobody cares about your ink. You just need to know where to look.
title: 7 Tattoo-Friendly Onsens in Rural Japan That You Won't Find on Google

content:
You just landed in Japan. You have an apartment lined up, a job offer in hand, and exactly zero ways to receive your salary. Welcome to one of the most frustrating rites of passage for every foreigner in Japan: opening a bank account.
Japan runs on cash and domestic bank transfers. Your landlord wants rent via bank transfer. Your employer pays into a Japanese bank account. Your phone plan, utilities, and gym membership all auto-debit from one. Without a Japanese bank account, you are essentially locked out of daily life.
But here is the problem: Japanese banks were not designed with foreigners in mind. Language barriers, strict residency requirements, and a general wariness toward short-term residents mean that many newcomers get turned away at the door, sometimes multiple times, before they finally get an account.
This guide breaks down exactly which banks to approach, what documents to bring, and how to avoid the most common rejection traps. Whether you arrived yesterday or have been here for years and are still using a workaround, this is the guide you need.
Note: Banking policies change frequently. Always verify current requirements directly with the bank before visiting.
Why Is It So Hard for Foreigners? Before diving into the how, it helps to understand the why. Japanese banks operate under strict anti-money laundering (AML) regulations, and the Financial Services Agency (FSA) has tightened rules over the past several years. Banks are required to verify identity, residency status, and in many cases, the expected length of stay in Japan.
This creates a catch-22 for newcomers: you need a bank account to function in Japan, but banks want proof that you plan to stay long enough to justify opening one. If your residence card shows less than six months remaining, many banks will refuse you outright.
Additionally, most bank branches operate entirely in Japanese. Even when a bank officially offers English services, the branch staff may not speak English, and the forms are almost always in Japanese. This is not a complaint; it is simply something you need to prepare for.
Major Banks Compared: Which One Should You Choose? Here is a practical breakdown of the major options available to foreigners in Japan in 2026.
Japan Post Bank (Yucho Ginko) Japan Post Bank is often the first recommendation for newcomers, and for good reason. It has the lowest barrier to entry among traditional banks.
Foreigner-friendliness: High. Japan Post Bank is generally the most welcoming to foreigners, including those who have been in Japan for less than six months. Required documents: Residence card (zairyu card), a form of secondary ID (health insurance card, My Number card, or passport with visa), and your seal (inkan) or signature. English support: Limited. Some larger branches in Tokyo and Osaka have English-speaking staff, but do not count on it. The Yucho Directo online banking platform has partial English support. Online banking: Available via Yucho Directo. The app and web interface have been gradually adding English functionality, though some sections remain Japanese-only. ATM network: Massive. Japan Post ATMs are everywhere, including rural areas. They also accept many international cards. Best for: Newcomers who just arrived, people in rural areas, anyone who wants the easiest approval process. Tip: Go to a larger post office (not the tiny neighborhood ones) for the best chance of finding staff who can help with foreign applications.
MUFG Bank (Mitsubishi UFJ Financial Group) MUFG is Japan’s largest bank by assets and has the most extensive branch and ATM network among the mega-banks.
Foreigner-friendliness: Moderate. MUFG generally requires at least six months of residency, though exceptions exist for corporate-sponsored accounts. Required documents: Residence card, passport, proof of address (utility bill or residence certificate from city hall), My Number notification, and an inkan or signature. English support: MUFG has made significant efforts in English support. Their Global Business Banking services and select branches in major cities offer English-speaking staff. The online banking portal has an English option. Online banking: MUFG Direct is available in English. The smartphone app supports English as well. ATM network: Very large, with partnerships across convenience store ATMs. Best for: People with stable employment at larger companies, anyone who needs a bank with strong international wire transfer capabilities. Mizuho Bank Mizuho is another mega-bank with wide coverage, though it has fewer branches than MUFG.
Foreigner-friendliness: Moderate to low. Mizuho tends to be stricter with residency requirements. Expect to need six months or more of residency. Required documents: Residence card, passport, proof of address, and an inkan (Mizuho branches are more likely to insist on an inkan rather than a signature). English support: Limited. A few branches in central Tokyo and Osaka have English-capable staff, but this is not widespread. Online banking has minimal English support. Online banking: Mizuho Direct is functional but primarily in Japanese. ATM network: Good coverage in urban areas, less so in rural regions. Best for: People whose employer specifically uses Mizuho for payroll (this is a common reason to open an account here). SMBC (Sumitomo Mitsui Banking Corporation) SMBC rounds out the trio of Japanese mega-banks.
Foreigner-friendliness: Moderate. Similar to MUFG and Mizuho, the six-month residency guideline applies in most cases. Required documents: Residence card, passport, proof of address, and inkan or signature. English support: SMBC has been improving its English services. The SMBC Direct online banking has some English support, and select branches offer English assistance. Online banking: Available with partial English support. ATM network: Strong coverage, especially through convenience store ATM partnerships. Best for: People whose employer banks with SMBC, or those in areas where SMBC branches are more convenient than alternatives. Rakuten Bank Rakuten Bank is an online-only bank and a popular choice for foreigners who already have some residency history.
Foreigner-friendliness: Moderate. Rakuten Bank accepts applications from foreigners, but the application process is entirely online and primarily in Japanese. You typically need a residence card with more than one year of remaining validity. Required documents: Residence card (photo upload), My Number, and a Japanese phone number. English support: The banking app and website have English options that have improved significantly. Customer support in English is available via email. Online banking: This is Rakuten Bank’s strength. Everything is managed online or through the app. No branch visits needed after setup. ATM network: Rakuten Bank partners with convenience store ATMs (Seven Bank, Lawson, E-net). Free ATM withdrawals are available depending on your account tier. Best for: Tech-savvy residents who prefer managing everything digitally, people already in the Rakuten ecosystem (Rakuten Card, Rakuten Mobile, etc.). Sony Bank Sony Bank is another online bank that has built a reputation for being foreigner-friendly.
Foreigner-friendliness: High. Sony Bank actively markets to international residents and offers one of the better English experiences among Japanese banks. Required documents: Residence card, My Number, and Japanese phone number. Application is done online. English support: Strong. The website, app, and customer support are available in English. Online banking: Excellent. Sony Bank’s app is well-designed and fully functional in English. Multi-currency accounts are available, making it useful for people who deal with multiple currencies. ATM network: Partners with convenience store ATMs. Free withdrawals available depending on account level. Best for: Foreigners who want a smooth English-language banking experience, people who send or receive money internationally, digital nomads with longer-term visas. Shinsei Bank (Now SBI Shinsei Bank) SBI Shinsei Bank has long been a favorite among the foreign community in Japan.
Foreigner-friendliness: High. Shinsei has historically been one of the most welcoming banks for foreign residents. Required documents: Residence card, passport, and Japanese phone number. Account opening can be done online or at select branches. English support: Good. English website and app are available. Some branches have English-speaking staff. Online banking: Solid online platform with English support. ATM network: Free ATM withdrawals at convenience stores depending on your account stage. Best for: Foreigners who want an account at a traditional bank with good English support, people who value free ATM access. Wise (Formerly TransferWise) Wise is not a Japanese bank, but it deserves mention because it solves a specific problem that many foreigners face.
What it offers: A multi-currency account with a Japanese yen balance. You get a local-style account number that can receive domestic transfers in some cases, though it is not a full Japanese bank account. Foreigner-friendliness: Very high. Wise is designed for international users. Limitations: Not all employers or services accept Wise account details for salary payments or auto-debits. It is not a replacement for a Japanese bank account, but it is an excellent complement. Best for: International money transfers, holding multiple currencies, bridging the gap before you open a proper Japanese bank account. Revolut Revolut launched in Japan and offers another digital banking option.
What it offers: A multi-currency account with a Japanese yen option. Similar to Wise in concept. Foreigner-friendliness: High, as it is designed for an international user base. Limitations: Like Wise, it may not be accepted for all domestic banking needs such as salary deposits and utility auto-debits. Verify current acceptance before relying on it. Best for: People who already use Revolut globally and want to keep their financial ecosystem consistent. Step-by-Step: Opening a Bank Account Right After Arrival Here is the most efficient path to getting a bank account as soon as possible after arriving in Japan.
Step 1: Get Your Residence Card You receive your residence card (zairyu card) at the airport when you land (for stays over 90 days) or at your local immigration office. This is your most important document. Without it, no bank will talk to you.
Step 2: Register Your Address at City Hall Within 14 days of moving into your residence, you must register your address at the local ward or city office (shiyakusho or kuyakusho). This is legally required and also gives you a proof of address. While there, ask for a juminhyo (residence certificate). You will need this for some banks.
Step 3: Get a Japanese Phone Number Almost every bank requires a Japanese phone number. Get a SIM card or phone plan as soon as possible. Budget options like Rakuten Mobile, Povo, LINEMO, or IIJmio work fine. The bank needs to be able to call or text you at this number.
Step 4: Consider Getting an Inkan (Personal Seal) While many banks now accept signatures from foreign customers, some branches still prefer or require an inkan. You can get a simple one made at a hanko shop for around 1,000 to 3,000 yen. It does not need to be elaborate. Having one ready removes a potential obstacle.
Step 5: Choose Your Bank and Visit Based on the comparison above, pick your target bank. For newcomers with less than six months of residency, Japan Post Bank is the safest bet.
What to bring:
Residence card Passport Proof of address (juminhyo or utility bill) Japanese phone number Inkan or be prepared to use your signature My Number card or notification letter (if you have it) Cash for an initial deposit (as little as 1 yen at Japan Post Bank, though 1,000 yen is a reasonable starting amount) Step 6: Visit During Quiet Hours Go to the bank on a weekday, ideally between 9:00 and 11:00 AM. Avoid month-end and the beginning of the month, as these are the busiest periods. Less crowded branches mean more time and patience from the staff to help with your application.
Step 7: Be Patient and Polite The process takes 30 to 60 minutes on average. If your Japanese is limited, having key phrases written down or using a translation app can help. Some people bring a Japanese-speaking friend for support, which is completely fine and often appreciated by the bank staff.
Step 8: Receive Your Cash Card Your bankbook (tsucho) is usually issued on the spot. Your cash card (ATM card) arrives by mail within one to two weeks. Make sure your mailbox has your name on it in the format the bank recorded, or the delivery may fail.
Common Rejection Reasons and How to Avoid Them Understanding why banks say no helps you avoid triggering those reasons.
“Your period of stay is too short.” This is the most common rejection. If your residence card shows less than six months remaining, most mega-banks will decline. Solution: Try Japan Post Bank first, as they are more flexible. Alternatively, wait until you renew your visa and try again.
“You do not have proof of address.” If you have not registered at city hall yet, you cannot prove where you live. Solution: Always register your address before visiting a bank.
“We cannot verify your identity.” This usually means the bank cannot match your documents or there is a discrepancy in name spellings between your passport and residence card. Solution: Make sure the name you provide matches exactly what is on your residence card.
“You do not have a Japanese phone number.” Banks need a way to reach you and increasingly use SMS verification. Solution: Get a Japanese phone number before visiting the bank.
“You already have an account at this bank.” Some banks limit foreigners to one account. If you had a previous account that was closed, this can cause issues. Solution: Bring any documentation related to previous accounts and explain the situation.
“This branch does not handle foreign applications.” Smaller branches sometimes redirect foreigners to larger branches. Solution: Call ahead or check the bank’s website for branches that specifically handle foreign customer applications.
The Digital Banking Alternative If traditional banks are giving you trouble, digital options can bridge the gap.
Wise lets you set up a Japanese yen balance quickly. While it is not a full domestic bank account, you can use it to receive international transfers and convert currencies at competitive rates. Some users have successfully used Wise details for certain domestic transfers, but this is not guaranteed for all services.
Revolut offers a similar proposition in Japan. The app-based experience is smooth, and if you are already a Revolut user from another country, adding a Japanese yen account is straightforward.
PayPay Bank (formerly Japan Net Bank) is another online option that some foreigners have used successfully, though the application process is primarily in Japanese.
These digital options work best as complements to a traditional Japanese bank account rather than replacements. For salary deposits, rent payments, and utility auto-debits, most services still require a standard domestic bank account.
Final Thoughts Opening a bank account in Japan as a foreigner is not impossible. It just requires the right preparation and the right bank. Here is the quick summary:
Just arrived, need an account fast: Go to Japan Post Bank. Want the best English experience online: Look at Sony Bank or SBI Shinsei Bank. Need a mega-bank for employer requirements: MUFG, SMBC, or Mizuho, depending on which your company uses. Want everything digital: Rakuten Bank once you have enough residency history. Need to move money internationally right now: Set up Wise or Revolut while you work on getting a domestic account. Get your residence card, register your address, get a phone number, and walk into a bank with confidence. The process might take an afternoon, but once it is done, daily life in Japan gets significantly easier.
Banking requirements and policies change. Always verify current requirements with the specific bank before applying. This guide reflects general practices as of early 2026.
summary:
You just landed in Japan. You have an apartment lined up, a job offer in hand, and exactly zero ways to receive your salary. Welcome to one of the most frustrating rites of passage for every foreigner in Japan: opening a bank account.

Japan runs on cash and domestic bank transfers. Your landlord wants rent via bank transfer. Your employer pays into a Japanese bank account. Your phone plan, utilities, and gym membership all auto-debit from one. Without a Japanese bank account, you are essentially locked out of daily life.
title: Opening a Bank Account in Japan as a Foreigner: The 2026 Ultimate Guide

content:
If you live in Japan as a foreign resident, you have probably heard about “My Number” at some point. Maybe your employer asked for it. Maybe you saw a poster at city hall. But many foreigners confuse two different things: the My Number itself and the My Number Card. They are not the same, and understanding the difference matters.
This guide explains everything you need to know about the My Number Card as a foreigner living in Japan in 2026, including why you should get one, how to apply, and what documents you need.
My Number vs. My Number Card: What Is the Difference? Your “My Number” (also called Individual Number) is a 12-digit number assigned to every resident of Japan, including foreign nationals registered in the Basic Resident Register. You received this number automatically when you registered your address at your local city or ward office. It was mailed to you on a paper notification card (tsuuchi card).
The “My Number Card” (Individual Number Card / Kojin Bangou Card) is a physical IC card with your photo, name, address, date of birth, and My Number printed on it. It also contains a digital certificate that lets you use online government services.
Here is the key distinction:
My Number = just a number (everyone has one) My Number Card = a physical ID card with IC chip (you must apply for it) The paper notification card that was mailed to you is NOT a My Number Card. Many foreigners keep the paper notification and think they are done. They are not. The actual card requires a separate application.
Why Should You Get a My Number Card? Getting a My Number Card is not legally mandatory, but it is practically becoming essential for daily life in Japan. Here are the concrete benefits:
1. Official Photo ID The My Number Card is accepted as government-issued photo identification throughout Japan. This is useful when you need ID but do not want to carry your residence card everywhere.
2. Health Insurance Card Replacement As of December 2024, Japan transitioned away from traditional paper/plastic health insurance cards. The My Number Card now functions as your health insurance card at hospitals and pharmacies. If you do not have one, you may need to use alternative certificates, which adds hassle.
3. Online Government Services (Mynaportal) With your My Number Card, you can access Mynaportal (myna.go.jp), the government’s online portal. Through Mynaportal you can:
Check your pension records View your tax information Complete certain administrative procedures online Link your health insurance information 4. Tax Filing (e-Tax) If you file taxes in Japan, you can use your My Number Card to file electronically through e-Tax. No more waiting in line at the tax office during filing season.
5. Convenience Store Services You can use your My Number Card at convenience store multi-copy machines to print official documents like your juminhyo (resident certificate) and tax certificates, even outside city hall business hours.
6. Faster Procedures at City Hall Many municipal offices have dedicated terminals where My Number Card holders can complete procedures faster.
7. Future Digital Services The Japanese government continues expanding My Number Card functionality. Having the card now means you will be ready for new services as they launch.
How to Apply: Step-by-Step There are several ways to apply for a My Number Card. The most common method for foreigners is the online application or applying at your local city/ward office.
Method 1: Online Application (Recommended) Step 1: Prepare Your Notification Card Find the paper notification (tsuuchi card) you received when you registered your address. It has a QR code and your application ID on it.
If you lost your notification card, go to your local city or ward office and ask for a new application ID (kojin bangou card koufu shinseisho).
Step 2: Take a Photo Take a photo that meets these requirements:
Taken within 6 months Facing forward, no hats or sunglasses White or light plain background Clear focus, no shadows on face Size: 3.5 cm x 4.5 cm (for mail) or 480 x 640 pixels minimum (for online) Tips: Use a white wall at home. Smartphone cameras work fine for the online application. Make sure the lighting is even and there are no shadows.
Step 3: Submit Online
Scan the QR code on your notification card It opens the application website Enter your email address You will receive an email with a link Click the link and upload your photo Confirm your information and submit The entire online process takes about 10 minutes.
Step 4: Wait for Your Card Processing usually takes 3 to 8 weeks, though times vary by municipality. You will receive a postcard (hagaki) in the mail telling you your card is ready for pickup.
Step 5: Pick Up Your Card at City Hall Go to your local city or ward office with:
The pickup notification postcard Your residence card (zairyu card) Your paper notification card (if you still have it) At the counter, you will set up two passwords:
A 4-digit numeric PIN (used for basic services) A 6-to-16 character alphanumeric password (used for digital certificates like e-Tax) Write these down and keep them safe. If you forget them, you must go back to city hall to reset them.
Method 2: Apply at City Hall If you prefer doing things in person:
Go to your local city or ward office Ask for the My Number Card application form Bring a photo (3.5 x 4.5 cm) or have one taken there (some offices offer this) Fill out the form and submit it Wait for the pickup notification (same 3-8 week timeline) Return to pick up your card and set your passwords Method 3: Apply by Mail Fill out the application form that came with your notification card Attach your photo Mail it using the prepaid envelope that was included Wait for the pickup notification Note: The prepaid envelope has an expiration date. If it has expired, ask your city office for a new application form and envelope.
Required Documents Here is a checklist of what you need:
For Application:
Notification card (tsuuchi card) or Application ID Photo meeting the requirements above Residence card (zairyu card) for reference For Pickup:
Pickup notification postcard (sent to your address) Residence card (zairyu card) Notification card (tsuuchi card), if you still have it If someone else picks up for you (limited cases only), additional documents are required. Check with your municipality for details.
Frequently Asked Questions How does the My Number Card relate to my Residence Card (Zairyu Card)? They are separate documents with different purposes:
Your Residence Card proves your immigration status and permission to stay in Japan Your My Number Card is for administrative and tax purposes You need both. The My Number Card does not replace your Residence Card. You are still legally required to carry your Residence Card at all times.
As of 2026, the Japanese government has been working on plans to eventually integrate Residence Card functions into the My Number Card system. Check the latest requirements with your local immigration office or city hall, as this integration is being phased in gradually.
What happens when my visa / period of stay is renewed? When you renew your visa or change your status of residence, you need to update the information on your My Number Card. Visit your local city or ward office after completing your immigration procedures. The digital certificate on your card will be updated.
Your My Number (the 12-digit number) stays the same. It does not change when you renew your visa.
What happens when I move to a different address? When you move within Japan:
Submit a moving-out notice (tenshutsu todoke) at your old city/ward office Submit a moving-in notice (tennyuu todoke) at your new city/ward office within 14 days Bring your My Number Card to the new office so they can update your address on the card The digital certificates on your card will be temporarily suspended during the move and reactivated at your new address.
I lost my notification card. Can I still apply? Yes. Go to your city or ward office and request a new application form. They can look up your My Number and issue new application materials.
Can I use my My Number Card at airports? There are gates at some Japanese airports that allow My Number Card holders to pass through more quickly for domestic identity verification purposes. This is separate from immigration/passport control.
What if I leave Japan permanently? If you move out of Japan, you submit a moving-out notice at your city office. Your My Number Card becomes invalid. If you return to Japan in the future and re-register as a resident, you will need to apply for a new card. Your My Number (the 12-digit number) will be the same as before.
Is there a fee? The initial My Number Card application is free. If you lose the card and need a replacement, there is a small fee (typically around 1,000 yen for the card and 200 yen for the electronic certificate, though amounts may vary by municipality).
How long is the card valid? For foreign residents: the card is valid until your current period of stay expires (the expiration date on your residence card) When you renew your visa, you also update your My Number Card The digital certificates have their own expiration cycle and may need to be renewed separately Check the latest requirements with your local city office, as policies on validity periods are subject to change.
Can I use my My Number Card for banking? Yes. Banks and financial institutions may ask for your My Number when you open an account or for certain financial transactions. Having the card makes this process smoother since it serves as both your ID and your My Number verification in one step.
What about privacy? Is it safe to carry? The IC chip on the card does not store your tax records, medical history, or bank account information. It only contains the basic information printed on the card (name, address, date of birth, My Number) and digital certificates for authentication. The card also has a privacy protection feature: the My Number on the card can be hidden using opaque material on some versions, and the IC chip has security measures against unauthorized reading.
That said, treat your My Number Card like any important ID. Do not share photos of it online and keep it secure.
Conclusion Getting a My Number Card is one of those tasks that is easy to put off but increasingly hard to live without in Japan. It takes about 10 minutes to apply online and a single trip to city hall to pick up. In return, you get a valid photo ID, access to online government services, easier tax filing, and the ability to use your health insurance seamlessly at hospitals.
If you have been in Japan for a while and still have not applied, now is a good time. The process is straightforward, it is free, and it will save you time and hassle in the long run.
Note: Government policies and procedures can change. Always check the latest requirements at your local city or ward office or on the official My Number portal. This guide reflects information available as of early 2026.
summary:
If you live in Japan as a foreign resident, you have probably heard about “My Number” at some point. Maybe your employer asked for it. Maybe you saw a poster at city hall. But many foreigners confuse two different things: the My Number itself and the My Number Card. They are not the same, and understanding the difference matters.

This guide explains everything you need to know about the My Number Card as a foreigner living in Japan in 2026, including why you should get one, how to apply, and what documents you need.
title: My Number Card for Foreigners in Japan: Complete 2026 Guide to Application & Benefits

content:
So you just landed in Japan, you’re standing at a convenience store, and the cashier is staring at you while you fumble through a pile of coins you don’t understand. Been there. The good news? Japan has gone through a massive cashless payment revolution over the past few years, and in 2026, you can get through most of daily life without touching a single yen coin – if you know which apps to set up.
Here’s the thing: Japan used to be one of the most cash-dependent developed nations on earth. Even in 2020, cash accounted for roughly 70% of all transactions. Fast forward to 2026, and the government’s push toward a cashless society has completely changed the landscape. QR code payments are everywhere. Transit cards work at most stores. Contactless credit cards are finally mainstream.
But as a foreigner, it’s not always obvious which services actually work for you, which ones require a Japanese bank account, and which ones are just going to waste your time. This guide breaks it all down.
The Big Picture: How Cashless Payments Work in Japan Before diving into specific apps, here’s what you need to know about the cashless ecosystem in Japan:
QR code payments (PayPay, LINE Pay, etc.) – You scan a store’s QR code or they scan yours. Dominant at small shops, restaurants, and izakayas. IC cards / transit cards (Suica, PASMO, ICOCA) – Tap-to-pay cards originally designed for trains but now accepted at convenience stores, vending machines, and many shops. Contactless credit/debit cards – Visa/Mastercard contactless (tap-to-pay) acceptance has expanded significantly. Mobile wallets (Apple Pay, Google Pay) – Link your cards and tap your phone. Works with both transit cards and credit cards. Japan-specific e-money (Rakuten Edy, nanaco, WAON) – Mostly tied to specific store chains. Less useful for foreigners. The key takeaway: no single payment method covers everything. You’ll want at least two or three options set up.
Cashless Payment Comparison: What Works for Foreigners in 2026 Here’s a breakdown of the major cashless payment options and how foreigner-friendly each one is.
PayPay Type: QR code payment Foreigner-friendly: Yes, with caveats Where it works: Practically everywhere – convenience stores, supermarkets, restaurants, taxis, small local shops, even some shrines Coverage: The single most widely accepted cashless payment in Japan. Over 60 million users. Setup for foreigners: Download the PayPay app (iOS/Android) Register with a Japanese phone number (you need at least a prepaid SIM or eSIM with a Japanese number) Verify your identity (passport + residence card for full features, or limited mode for tourists) How to charge: Link a Japanese bank account (if you have one) Charge at convenience store ATMs (7-Eleven, Lawson, FamilyMart) using cash – this is the easiest method for newcomers Some international credit cards can be linked, but support varies – verify current compatibility Pros: Accepted almost everywhere, frequent promotional campaigns with point-back deals Cons: Requires a Japanese phone number, full features need identity verification Best for: Daily shopping, restaurants, splitting bills with Japanese friends Suica Type: IC transit card / e-money Foreigner-friendly: Very Where it works: All trains/buses in major metro areas, convenience stores, vending machines, many restaurants and shops Coverage: Accepted at most places displaying the IC card logo (interoperable with PASMO, ICOCA, and other regional IC cards) Setup for foreigners: Physical card: Available at JR station ticket machines (deposit of 500 yen, refundable). Note: physical Suica cards have had limited availability in recent years due to chip shortages – check current stock at stations Mobile Suica (iPhone): Add directly through the Wallet app on iPhone 8 or later. Works even without a Japanese credit card – you can add money via Apple Pay with an international Visa/Mastercard Mobile Suica (Android): Available through Google Pay on phones with FeliCa chips. Most Android phones sold outside Japan do NOT have FeliCa. Google Pixel 7 and later models sold globally do support it – verify your device How to charge: At station ticket machines (cash) Through the mobile app (linked credit card) At convenience store registers (cash – just ask “Suica ni charge shitai desu”) Pros: Super fast tap-and-go, no need to unlock your phone, works offline on trains Cons: Maximum balance of 20,000 yen, not accepted at as many small shops as PayPay Best for: Transportation, convenience stores, vending machines, quick purchases PASMO Type: IC transit card / e-money Foreigner-friendly: Very (same as Suica) Where it works: Same places as Suica – they’re fully interchangeable at payment terminals Key difference from Suica: Issued by private railway companies in Tokyo (Suica is JR East). Functionally identical for payments. Setup: Same process as Suica. Mobile PASMO also available on iPhone. Should you get Suica or PASMO? Either one works. Most foreigners default to Suica because it has slightly broader name recognition and Mobile Suica was available first. If you commute on a private railway line (Tokyu, Keio, Odakyu, etc.), PASMO commuter passes might be more convenient. Best for: Same use cases as Suica Credit Cards (Visa / Mastercard / Amex) Type: Contactless + chip-and-pin Foreigner-friendly: Yes Where it works: Major chains, department stores, electronics stores, hotels, many restaurants. Acceptance at small independent shops is hit-or-miss. Setup: Bring your existing international credit card. No additional setup needed for chip transactions. For contactless tap-to-pay, add your card to Apple Pay or Google Pay. Important notes: Visa and Mastercard have the best acceptance in Japan Amex works at many places but not as widely JCB is a Japanese brand – great acceptance domestically, less so abroad Discover/Diners Club have limited acceptance Some older terminals still only accept chip-and-pin, not contactless Always carry at least one physical card as backup Foreign transaction fees: Check with your card issuer. Cards with no foreign transaction fees will save you 1-3% on every purchase Best for: Larger purchases, hotels, department stores, online shopping Apple Pay / Google Pay Type: Mobile wallet Foreigner-friendly: Yes Where it works: Anywhere that accepts contactless credit cards or Suica/PASMO Setup: Apple Pay: Add your credit/debit card through the Wallet app. You can also add a Suica or PASMO card directly. Google Pay: Add your card through the Google Wallet app. Mobile Suica support requires a FeliCa-enabled device (see Suica section above). Pro tip: With Apple Pay, you can set Suica as your “Express Transit” card, which lets you tap through train gates without unlocking your phone or authenticating. This is a game-changer during rush hour. Best for: Combining transit and shopping payments in one device LINE Pay Type: QR code payment Foreigner-friendly: Partially Where it works: Convenience stores, many chain restaurants, drugstores Current status: LINE Pay has been scaling back its standalone payment service. Many of its functions are being integrated into other services. Its acceptance footprint is smaller than PayPay. Setup: Download LINE (the messaging app), then activate LINE Pay within it. Requires a Japanese phone number. How to charge: Convenience store cash charge, linked bank account, or LINE Pay card Should you bother? Honestly, if you already have PayPay, LINE Pay is redundant for most foreigners. The main reason to have LINE is for messaging (everyone in Japan uses it), and LINE Pay is just a bonus feature inside the app. Best for: People who are already heavy LINE users Rakuten Pay Type: QR code payment Foreigner-friendly: Limited Where it works: Many chain stores, convenience stores, drugstores Setup: Requires a Rakuten account and a linked credit card. The app is available internationally but works best with a Rakuten credit card (which requires Japanese residency to apply for). Best for: People who are deep in the Rakuten ecosystem (Rakuten Mobile, Rakuten Card, Rakuten Ichiba shopping) Other Options You Might See nanaco: Primarily for 7-Eleven and Ito-Yokado stores. Not worth setting up unless you shop there constantly. WAON: Primarily for AEON group stores. Same situation. Rakuten Edy: Declining in relevance. Skip it. d Barai: NTT Docomo’s QR payment. Only useful if you have a Docomo phone plan. au PAY: KDDI’s QR payment. Only useful if you have an au phone plan. Merpay: Tied to the Mercari marketplace app. Niche use case. The 3 Apps Every Foreigner Should Set Up First If you’re just arriving in Japan and feeling overwhelmed, here’s your priority list:
1. Mobile Suica (via Apple Pay or Google Pay) Set this up on the plane. Seriously. You can create a Mobile Suica in Apple Wallet before you even land. Add 2,000-3,000 yen to start. The moment you step off the plane, you can tap through the train gates at Narita or Haneda and ride directly into the city. No fumbling at ticket machines, no lines, no confusion.
You’ll use Suica multiple times every single day – trains, buses, convenience stores, vending machines, coin lockers. It’s the backbone of getting around Japan.
2. PayPay Set this up on your first day. Once you have a Japanese phone number (even a prepaid SIM works), download PayPay and register. Load it up with cash at a convenience store ATM. PayPay fills the gaps that Suica and credit cards don’t cover – that tiny ramen shop, the local izakaya, the street food vendor at a festival, the barbershop in your neighborhood.
3. Your Existing Credit/Debit Card via Apple Pay or Google Pay Already done if you set up Suica. Make sure at least one international credit or debit card is added to your mobile wallet. This covers department stores, electronics shops, hotels, and online purchases. Having it on your phone means you can tap-to-pay at contactless terminals without digging out your wallet.
Bonus: If you plan to stay long-term, eventually get a Japanese credit card (Rakuten Card is one of the easier ones to apply for as a foreign resident). This opens up more options and earns you points in the Japanese rewards ecosystem.
Common Problems and How to Fix Them “My credit card got declined” Reason 1: Your home bank flagged the transaction as suspicious. Call your bank before your trip and let them know you’ll be in Japan. Reason 2: The terminal only accepts certain brands. Ask “Visa wa tsukaemasuka?” (Can I use Visa?) Reason 3: Some older terminals don’t support contactless. Try inserting the chip instead. “I can’t register for PayPay without a Japanese phone number” You need a Japanese phone number. Options:
Buy a prepaid SIM at the airport (IIJmio, Mobal, and others offer data + voice SIMs) Get an eSIM that includes a Japanese phone number (not all eSIMs do – data-only eSIMs won’t work) If you’re a resident, sign up for a regular phone plan (Rakuten Mobile, ahamo, LINEMO, etc.) “Mobile Suica isn’t working at the gate” Make sure your phone has enough battery. If your iPhone dies, Express Transit mode has a small power reserve, but don’t rely on it. Check that Suica is set as your Express Transit card in Settings > Wallet & Apple Pay. Make sure you’re tapping the top of your phone (where the NFC antenna is) flat against the reader. If you get an error, check your Suica balance. Minimum fare balance is needed to enter the gate. “The store says cash only” Yes, this still happens. Some traditional restaurants, small family-run shops, and certain institutions are cash-only even in 2026. Always carry 5,000-10,000 yen in cash as backup. You can withdraw cash from ATMs at 7-Eleven (7 Bank) and Japan Post – these are the most reliable ATMs for international cards.
“I got charged in yen but the amount looks wrong on my statement” Your card issuer converts the yen charge to your home currency using their exchange rate, plus any foreign transaction fee (usually 1-3%). The exchange rate used might differ from what you see on Google. This is normal. To minimize fees, use a card with no foreign transaction fees.
“Which convenience store ATMs work with foreign cards?” 7-Eleven (7 Bank ATM): Most reliable for international cards. English interface available. Lawson (Lawson Bank ATM): Also supports international cards. FamilyMart: E-net ATMs generally support international cards. Japan Post ATM: Reliable for international withdrawals. Found in post offices everywhere. Pro Tips from People Who’ve Been There Train station kiosks (NewDays, Kiosk): Always accept Suica. Fastest way to grab a drink or snack. Vending machines: Most now accept Suica/IC cards. Look for the IC card reader (usually on the right side). Tap, select your drink, done in 3 seconds. Tax-free shopping: When buying tax-free at electronics stores or department stores (purchases over 5,000 yen by tourists), you usually need to pay with a credit card or cash. PayPay and Suica purchases may not qualify for tax-free – ask the store. Splitting bills: PayPay has a bill-splitting feature. If your Japanese friends use PayPay, you can send money directly to their accounts. Budgeting: Load a fixed amount onto Suica or PayPay each week. When it runs out, you know you’ve hit your spending limit. Much easier than tracking individual credit card charges. Receipts: Say “reshiito onegaishimasu” if you need a receipt. Cashless transactions still generate receipts at most stores. Points and rewards: PayPay gives you points on purchases that can be used like cash. Suica doesn’t have a standalone points system, but JRE POINT can be linked to your Suica for rewards at JR East stations. Quick Reference: Where Each Payment Method Works Best Situation Best Payment Trains and buses Suica / PASMO Convenience stores Suica or PayPay (both work everywhere) Small restaurants / izakayas PayPay Department stores Credit card Vending machines Suica Online shopping Credit card Supermarkets PayPay or credit card Taxis PayPay, credit card, or Suica (varies by company) Shrines / temples (admission, charms) Cash (some now accept PayPay) Street food / festivals Cash or PayPay Hotels Credit card Drugstores PayPay, credit card, or Suica Coin lockers at stations Suica Final Thoughts Japan’s cashless payment landscape can feel overwhelming at first glance, but here’s the simple version: Suica for transit and quick taps, PayPay for everything else, credit card for big purchases, and a bit of cash for the holdouts. That four-part combo will get you through 99% of daily life.
Set up Mobile Suica before you even arrive. Download PayPay on day one. Keep your credit card in your mobile wallet. Carry a few thousand yen in cash. You’re good.
The best part? Once you’re set up, paying for things in Japan becomes incredibly smooth. A quick tap here, a QR scan there, and you’re on your way. No more panicking at the register trying to count out exact change in coins you can barely read.
Welcome to cashless Japan. Your wallet (and your coin purse) will thank you.
Note: App features, pricing, and availability may change. Verify current details on each service’s official website before relying on specific features. Information reflects conditions as of early 2026.
summary: So you just landed in Japan, you’re standing at a convenience store, and the cashier is staring at you while you fumble through a pile of coins you don’t understand. Been there. The good news? Japan has gone through a massive cashless payment revolution over the past few years, and in 2026, you can get through most of daily life without touching a single yen coin – if you know which apps to set up.
title: Mastering Cashless Payments in Japan: A Foreigner's Guide to PayPay, Suica & More (2026)
